conflict_summary: Agent 365 suggests a control-plane framing centered on observability and governance, while Magentic-UI suggests a human-centered mixed-initiative interaction framing.
possible_explanations: These are solving different levels of the stack: fleet oversight versus task-level human collaboration.
what_would_resolve_it: Decide explicitly whether humanctl owns fleet supervision or human unblock loops.

conflict_summary: AG-UI allows open-ended generative UI and shared state, while Copilot Workspace emphasizes staged editable flow.
possible_explanations: Open-ended protocol primitives do not require an unstructured product UX; humanctl can use structured surfaces on top of flexible payloads.
what_would_resolve_it: Choose whether v1 is optimized for general layout freedom or for staged asks/artifacts/watches.

conflict_summary: Interruption research warns about stress costs, while Magentic-UI still includes proactive user interruptions for clarifications and approvals.
possible_explanations: The tension is resolved if interruptions are rare, high-value, and easy to dismiss or defer.
what_would_resolve_it: Define explicit escalation levels and thresholds for when live interruption is acceptable.

conflict_summary: Local-first principles put the primary copy on the local device, while control-plane products are cloud-centric and centralized.
possible_explanations: These approaches optimize for different trust and deployment models.
what_would_resolve_it: Decide whether humanctl is fundamentally local infrastructure or a hosted coordination service.

conflict_summary: Magentic-UI emphasizes plans, approvals, and tasks, while the current humanctl concept has a very generic `thing` abstraction.
possible_explanations: A generic internal abstraction may still coexist with more semantic user-facing objects.
what_would_resolve_it: Decide whether `thing` remains internal while `ask`, `artifact`, and `watch` become product-facing terms.
